pointsAnchor.footnotePercentile: Percentile compares your average opponent points to every nation in the current FIFA ranking snapshot (higher average = harder path).
pointsAnchor.footnoteAnchor: The ≈ team is the closest single-country match by points — shorthand for the average, not your actual opponent list.

teamAnalysis.title: Team path analysis
teamAnalysis.subtitle: See how tough a team's road to the final is, based on FIFA ranking points of every opponent faced.

advanced.title: Advanced statistics
advanced.subtitle: Deeper opponent distribution and cohort ordering metrics for statistical comparison.
advanced.pointsChartAria: Opponent FIFA points chart. Mean {mean}, population standard deviation {deviation}.
advanced.pointsChartHint: The shaded band spans one population standard deviation around the mean ({lower}–{upper}). Bars outside it are unusually far from this path's average opponent.
advanced.cohortOrderingHint: Across {count} comparable teams at {stage} or beyond, how closely do points-based and rank-based path orderings match?
advanced.footnoteOpponentStats: Opponent distribution stats use population variance over the matches included in your stage filter (n is shown in each block).
advanced.correlationSectionTitle: Understanding Spearman ρ and Kendall τ
advanced.spearmanBody: Spearman ρ (rho) measures how similarly teams are ordered when ranked by average opponent points versus average opponent rank. A value of 1 means both methods produce the exact same ranking — the hardest paths are the same either way. A value of 0 means the two orderings are unrelated: knowing a team's position by points tells you nothing about their position by rank. Values near 1 (typical here) mean the two metrics mostly agree, with only small swaps among similar paths.
advanced.kendallBody: Kendall τ-b (tau-b) answers the same question from a different angle: it counts pairwise agreements between the two orderings, adjusting for ties in either list. For every pair of teams, do points and rank agree on which path is harder? τ = 1 means every pair agrees; τ = 0 means agreement and disagreement balance out. It is especially useful when several teams have similar averages and ties are common.
advanced.correlationWhyItMatters: Why this matters: CupPath uses points as the primary difficulty metric because FIFA points capture real strength gaps. Rank is ordinal — each position step counts equally. These coefficients show how much the alternate rank-based view diverges from the points-based one. When ρ and τ stay high, both stories align; when they dip, a few teams may shift position depending on which metric you prefer.
advanced.footnotePrimary: Primary path difficulty and the gauge still use average opponent FIFA points.

groups.title: World Cup groups
groups.subtitle: All 48 teams in their draw groups with FIFA ranking and group standings
groups.detailSubtitle: Standings and FIFA strength for the selected group
groups.fifaPointsChartHint: Bars show each team's FIFA points. The dashed orange line is this group's mean; colored lines mark the weakest group, strongest group, and tournament average.
groups.statsNote: Statistics use all teams in the group with available FIFA data. Variance and standard deviation use the population formulas (n = 4).
groups.pathStatsNote: Rivals columns use group-stage opponent averages across the full tournament path. Path # ranks all 48 teams by average opponent points.

advanced.title: Group strength comparison
advanced.subtitle: Compare all 12 groups by average FIFA strength and see how points-based and rank-based orderings agree.
advanced.tableHint: Click a column to sort. Click a row to select that group.
advanced.groupOrderingHint: Across all {count} groups, comparing orderings by average group FIFA points (higher = stronger) and average group FIFA rank (lower = stronger).
advanced.groupOrderingIntro: Each group is scored by the average FIFA strength of its four teams. Rank 1 is the strongest group on that scale.
advanced.spearmanBody: Spearman ρ measures how similarly groups are ordered by average points versus average rank. 1 means identical order; 0 means unrelated; values near 1 mean both methods agree on which groups are strongest.
advanced.kendallBody: Kendall τ-b counts pairwise agreement between the two group orderings, adjusting for ties. τ = 1 means every pair of groups ranks the same way under both metrics; τ = 0 means mixed agreement.
advanced.footnotePrimary: Group cards show each group's strength rank. Path difficulty per team is in the detail table and on Team path.

about.title: About CupPath
about.methodologyBody:
CupPath measures how difficult each team's World Cup path is using FIFA ranking points. Higher opponent points mean a harder match. Average path difficulty is the unweighted mean of opponent points across the rounds you choose to include.

For teams still in the tournament, scheduled (unplayed) opponents on the current path count toward the average, valued at the selected FIFA snapshot. Eliminated teams are measured only over the matches they played.

A team is marked Eliminated after a knockout loss (including extra time and penalties) or when it fails to advance from the group stage (top two per group plus the eight best third-place teams).
about.pointsVsRankBody:
CupPath ranks path difficulty by average opponent FIFA points (higher = harder). We also show average opponent FIFA rank (lower = harder) for context.

The two orderings can differ slightly. FIFA points are a continuous strength measure; rank is an ordinal position where every step counts the same. A path with one elite opponent may rank higher by points than by average rank, because that opponent's points gap is larger than their rank number suggests.

The # column on Compare and the hardest-path rank on Team path use points. On Compare you can sort the table by average rank to see the alternate ordering.
about.comparisonTitle: Why eliminated teams are not ranked together
about.comparisonBody:
The # column on Compare—and the hardest-path rank on Team path—only compare teams that reached the same stage of the tournament as the rounds included in your averages.

That is intentional. A side knocked out in the group played three group matches. A quarter-finalist may have played five or more, often against stronger opposition. Mixing those paths into one leaderboard would compare incomparable averages.

Eliminated teams still appear in the comparison table with their averages from the matches they actually played, marked as Eliminated. They simply do not receive a # rank unless they belong to the cohort at your selected stage—for example, if you only count group-stage matches, all 48 teams are comparable again.

Tied averages receive the same # rank (competition ranking). While groups are still playing, provisional group leaders may count toward knockout cohorts before all three matchdays finish.
about.pointsGapBody: Points gap is opponent FIFA points minus team FIFA points. A positive gap means a harder opponent (stronger on paper). A negative gap means an easier opponent. This is the primary per-match difficulty signal on the path table.
about.rankGapBody: Rank gap is opponent rank minus team rank. A negative gap means a harder opponent (they are ranked higher). A positive gap means an easier opponent.
about.advancedStatsBody:
On Team path, the Advanced statistics section is always visible below the path table. It opens with an opponent points chart: one bar per opponent (flag and FIFA code below), with values shown at the bottom of each bar.

The chart includes a shaded band for mean ± one population standard deviation over the included matches, a dashed orange line for the mean, and a solid blue line for the selected team's own FIFA points — so you can see each opponent relative to the path average and to the team itself.

Below the chart, compact tables summarize opponent FIFA points and opponent FIFA rank (median, standard deviation, min, max). A cohort ordering block shows Spearman ρ and Kendall τ-b: how closely the points-based and rank-based path orderings agree among comparable teams at your selected stage.

Distribution stats use population variance over the matches in your stage filter (n is the match count). The primary difficulty gauge and path # still use average opponent FIFA points.

On Groups, a separate collapsible advanced panel shows similar correlation metrics for group strength ordering among the twelve groups.
about.headToHeadBody:
On Compare, the head-to-head panel lets you pick any two teams and see their path difficulty side by side: average opponent points, average opponent rank, path #, and status.

The Δ column shows Team A minus Team B. A positive points delta means Team A faced tougher opponents on average. Path # is only shown when both teams belong to the comparison cohort for your stage filter — otherwise the panel explains that the rank is unavailable.
about.groupsBody:
The Groups page ranks all twelve World Cup groups by average FIFA strength of their four teams. Each group card shows a strength rank (# / 12) by average points and by average rank, plus a mini table with qualification colors (1st, 2nd, best third).

Selecting a group opens a detail table with standings, FIFA points and rank, and each team's path averages (avg opponent points, avg opponent rank, path # among all 48 teams). Group-level statistics blocks show FIFA points and rank distributions across the four teams.
about.stagesBody:
Count matches from lets you choose which rounds feed the averages—group only, through the final, or anything in between. The furthest round selected defines the comparison cohort for the # rank.

On Compare, Show teams narrows the table to sides that reached at least that round. You cannot show teams eliminated before the furthest round in your averages, because they never played those matches. Your ranking-mode choice is remembered when you switch between Team path, Groups, Compare, and Simulate.
about.simulationTitle: Path simulation — what it does and what it does not
about.simulationBody:
Simulate is an exploratory tool. It lets you swap group positions, pick alternate knockout winners, and see how a team's average opponent difficulty might change. It is not a match predictor and does not model form, injuries, or head-to-head tiebreakers beyond what is described below.

Group edits reorder teams within their existing group only—you cannot move a nation into a different group. When you swap positions, each team keeps its actual group-stage record (points, goal difference, goals scored). The simulator does not replay group matches to invent new stats.

Best third-place qualification ranks all twelve third-place teams by group-stage record: points, then goal difference, then goals scored, then team code as a final tie-break. This is a simplified version of FIFA rules. Fair-play points, head-to-head among thirds, and drawing of lots are not modeled.

The Best thirds ranking tab shows who would advance under those rules. The knockout bracket, however, still fills each third-place slot (3A, 3D, etc.) from whoever sits third in that group—it does not yet reassign which qualifying third goes to which Round of 32 fixture using FIFA's full assignment matrix. So a third marked Out in the ranking tab may still appear in a bracket slot, and vice versa.

Knockout overrides change winners match by match. Unplayed future opponents still use FIFA ranking points from your selected snapshot—not results from your simulated bracket. Treat simulated path difficulty as a what-if on opponent identity, not as a forecast of tournament outcomes.
about.dataBody: Match data comes from openfootball/worldcup.json (2026), stored in Vercel Blob and refreshed via a daily production cron (once per day on Vercel Hobby). Scores, results, and next opponents update from there. FIFA rankings come from the World Football Ranking API on RapidAPI, also stored in Blob and refreshed on the same schedule. On a Pro plan you can add more frequent crons (for example hourly live rankings). Bundled seed JSON in the repo is used when Blob is unavailable.
about.rankingModesBody:
19 November 2025 is the ranking FIFA used to seed the World Cup draw. On that date each nation was assigned to a pot before groups were drawn, so this snapshot answers how strong your opponents looked on paper before the tournament started.

We also offer snapshots from 19 January, 1 April, and 11 June 2026—useful for tracking how opponent strength looked at different points in the cycle—and Live mode, which uses the current FIFA ranking and refreshes on the production cron schedule (daily by default; hourly if you configure an additional cron on Pro).

home.title: Tournament snapshot
home.subtitle: Interesting facts from the World Cup draw and results — average strength by round, path difficulty, upsets, and teams beating expectations.

snapshot.teamsRemainingHint: Still alive at this stage or beyond

cohorts.title: Strength by stage
cohorts.footnote: Path difficulty is the mean opponent FIFA points on each team's road through that stage (played + scheduled).

groupStagePool.title: Group stage pool
groupStagePool.subtitle: FIFA strength and rival difficulty across all {count} teams in the group phase.
groupStagePool.avgGroupRivalDifficultyHint: Mean opponent FIFA points across each team's three group matches.
groupStagePool.lowestRankedQualifierHint: Advanced from Group {group} with the worst pre-tournament FIFA rank.

matchOutcomeGap.title: Results vs FIFA points gap
matchOutcomeGap.subtitle: How often the favorite wins, shares points, or loses an upset — grouped by how far apart the teams were on paper.
matchOutcomeGap.noMatches: No played matches in the selected rounds.
matchOutcomeGap.closeGapInsight: Within 25 FIFA points ({count} matches): {winPct}% favorite wins, {drawPct}% draws, {lossPct}% upsets.
matchOutcomeGap.chartAria: Stacked outcome frequencies and per-match dots for {count} matches by FIFA points gap.
matchOutcomeGap.interactionHint: Scroll to zoom match dots. Drag to pan.
matchOutcomeGap.footnote: From the higher-rated team's view. Group matches use full-time score only. In knockout ties, extra-time wins count as wins; matches decided on penalties count as draws.
matchOutcomeGap.outlierFootnote: Outlier rings mark draws or upsets whose FIFA points gap is at or above that stage's mean + 1 SD — the same idea as each round's deep dive. Small rounds make that threshold noisy.

highlights.title: Highlights
highlights.overPerformerFootnote: Furthest run vs pre-tournament seed (FIFA rank mapped to expected stage).
highlights.upsetFootnote: Based on FIFA points gap at kickoff (positive gap = opponent rated higher).
highlights.giantKillerFootnote: Most total FIFA points overcome in wins vs higher-rated opponents.
highlights.groupOfDeathFootnote: Group with the highest average FIFA points among its four teams.

home.methodologyNote: Metrics use the selected FIFA snapshot and current bracket state.

groupExpectedFinishes.title: Group stage deep dive
groupExpectedFinishes.subtitle: From win/loss and draws, through the final tables, to who drew the toughest rivals.
groupExpectedFinishes.storyWinLossLead: When someone won, did the FIFA favorite hold? Scan every win/loss for upsets and how wide the rating gap was.
groupExpectedFinishes.storyDrawsLead: Draws show up when teams were rated even—or when a favorite only shared the points. See which ties carried the biggest gap on paper.
groupExpectedFinishes.storyStandingsLead: Three matchdays later: who finished below their pre-tournament seed, and who paid for it with elimination?
groupExpectedFinishes.storyDifficultyLead: Not all groups are equal. Compare rival strength, qualification rates above and below the average, and the biggest outliers.
groupExpectedFinishes.biggestUnderdogDrawHint: Lower-rated team held on for a draw · largest FIFA points gap
groupExpectedFinishes.biggestUnderdogWinHint: Lower-rated team won · largest FIFA points gap
groupExpectedFinishes.evenMatchesHint: Equal FIFA points — draw expected (0 pt gap)
groupExpectedFinishes.favoriteHitRateHint: Among {count} matches with a clear favorite
groupExpectedFinishes.positionLadderHint: Dashed slot = paper finish; filled slot = actual. Highlighted cards = eliminated from the group. Sorted by places dropped.
groupExpectedFinishes.drawGapChartCaption: FIFA points gap distribution for drawn group matches
groupExpectedFinishes.drawGapChartFootnote: Small sample: one draw can shift SD a lot. Outliers are draws with gap above mean + 1 SD and gap greater than zero.
groupExpectedFinishes.drawsTableAboveMeanHint: Teal rows: gap above the average for draws.
groupExpectedFinishes.winLossTableUnderdogHint: Orange rows: the lower-rated team won (upset on paper), regardless of gap size.
groupExpectedFinishes.winLossTableUpsetHint: Upset badge: the lower-rated team won with a gap at or above mean + 1 SD — an unusually large rating gap for a decisive match.
groupExpectedFinishes.winLossTableGapHint: Gap is the FIFA points difference between the two teams; a bigger gap means a clearer paper favorite.
groupExpectedFinishes.winLossGapChartCaption: FIFA points gap distribution for decisive group matches
groupExpectedFinishes.winLossGapChartFootnote: Small sample: SD can shift quickly. Orange dots and rows mark underdog wins; orange rings mark the Upset badge (mean + 1 SD).
groupExpectedFinishes.groupDifficultySubtitle: {count} teams · team FIFA points vs average rival difficulty
groupExpectedFinishes.groupDifficultyCaption: Group-stage team strength vs average rival difficulty
groupExpectedFinishes.groupDifficultyFootnote: Each dot is one team. X = team FIFA points; Y = mean FIFA points of that team's three group opponents. Green = advanced; red = eliminated.
groupExpectedFinishes.groupDifficultyReadingFootnote: Further right = stronger team; higher on the chart = stronger rival.
groupExpectedFinishes.groupDifficultyRateGapHint: Teams on easier draws qualified {gap} pp more often ({direction})
groupExpectedFinishes.groupDifficultyAtMeanNote: {count} teams sat on the tournament avg ({mean}); {qualified} advanced.
groupExpectedFinishes.tiebreakerFootnote: Paper results: higher FIFA points expected to win; equal points expected to draw. Paper standings use expected points, then FIFA points — no invented scorelines. Actual tables use points, goal difference, and goals scored; real FIFA tiebreakers (head-to-head, etc.) may differ.

knockoutStage.gapChartFootnote: Small sample: SD can shift quickly. Orange dots mark underdog qualifications; orange rings mark the Upset badge (mean + 1 SD).
knockoutStage.opponentDifficultyReadingFootnote: Further right = stronger team; higher on the chart = stronger rival.
knockoutStage.opponentDifficultyAboveMeanHint: Teams that faced a harder-than-average opponent
knockoutStage.opponentDifficultyBelowMeanHint: Teams that faced an easier-than-average opponent
knockoutStage.opponentDifficultyRateGapHint: Teams on easier ties qualified {gap} pp more often ({direction})
knockoutStage.opponentDifficultyAtMeanNote: {count} teams sat on the Round avg ({mean}); {qualified} qualified.

factsTables.underdogRowHint: Orange rows: the lower-rated team won (upset on paper).
factsTables.upsetRowHint: Upset badge: the lower-rated team won with a gap at or above mean + 1 SD — an unusually large rating gap for a decisive match.

roundOf32.title: Round of 32
roundOf32.subtitle: FIFA strength and rival difficulty across the {count} teams in the Round of 32.
roundOf32.poolLowestRankedQualifierHint: Qualified for the Round of 16 against {opponent}.
roundOf32.gapChartCaption: FIFA points gap distribution for Round of 32 ties
roundOf32.opponentDifficultySubtitle: {count} teams · team FIFA points vs opponent difficulty
roundOf32.opponentDifficultyCaption: Round of 32 team strength vs opponent difficulty
roundOf32.opponentDifficultyFootnote: Each dot is one team. X = team FIFA points; Y = Round of 32 opponent FIFA points. Green = victory; red = defeat.

roundOf16.title: Round of 16
roundOf16.subtitle: FIFA strength and rival difficulty across the {count} teams in the Round of 16.
roundOf16.gapChartCaption: FIFA points gap distribution for Round of 16 ties
roundOf16.opponentDifficultySubtitle: {count} teams · team FIFA points vs opponent difficulty
roundOf16.opponentDifficultyCaption: Round of 16 team strength vs opponent difficulty
roundOf16.opponentDifficultyFootnote: Each dot is one team. X = team FIFA points; Y = Round of 16 opponent FIFA points. Green = victory; red = defeat.

quarterFinal.title: Quarter-finals
quarterFinal.subtitle: FIFA strength and rival difficulty across the {count} teams in the quarter-finals.
quarterFinal.gapChartCaption: FIFA points gap distribution for quarter-final ties
quarterFinal.opponentDifficultySubtitle: {count} teams · team FIFA points vs opponent difficulty
quarterFinal.opponentDifficultyCaption: Quarter-final team strength vs opponent difficulty
quarterFinal.opponentDifficultyFootnote: Each dot is one team. X = team FIFA points; Y = quarter-final opponent FIFA points. Green = victory; red = defeat.

simulate.title: Path simulation
simulate.subtitle: Pick alternate knockout winners to see how a team's path difficulty would change. Each side shows its bracket slot (e.g. 1J · Argentina vs 2H · Cape Verde).
simulate.pathCompareFootnote: Δ sim compares the simulated path to the focus team's actual path. Positive points = harder; for rank, negative = harder.
simulate.pathCompareFootnoteWithComparison: Δ sim compares simulated vs the focus team's actual path. Δ comp compares simulated vs the comparison team's actual path. Positive points = harder; for rank, negative = harder.
simulate.averagesIncludeScheduled: Averages include played matches plus scheduled opponents still on the path (using the current FIFA snapshot).
simulate.harderBy: Simulated path is {gap} harder by average opponent points.
simulate.easierBy: Simulated path is {gap} easier by average opponent points.
simulate.bracketHint: Tap a team to set them as the winner for that match. Orange slots show where each side comes from in the draw.
simulate.pickWinnersAlertBody: Group changes altered played matches. Choose winners for {count, plural, one {# match} other {# matches}} to continue the simulated path.

bracket.pickWinner: Tap a team to simulate them winning this match.

simulate.pathChartAria: Opponent FIFA points along actual and simulated paths for {team}
simulate.pathChartAriaWithComparison: Opponent FIFA points along actual, simulated, and comparison paths for {team}
simulate.noPathChanges: Simulation changes the bracket but not this team's opponents yet.
simulate.groupFinishesHint: Swap positions to simulate a different draw slot. Match records (points, GD, GF) stay from actual results—only the table order changes.
simulate.sortByPoints: Sort all groups by FIFA points

bestThird.subtitle: All 12 third-place teams ranked by group-stage record. The top 8 advance.
bestThird.footnote: Tie-break order: points, then goal difference, then goals scored, then team code. Stats come from actual group-stage results; swapping positions does not recalculate match records.
